metadata.title: Three Levels of AI Supervision — The Sheridan-Verplank Framework

beats.title: Liam cold open — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: The usage-versus-supervision grid clarified why Priya's Interaction B, Level II capability with Level I supervision, sits dangerously below the diagonal. I want to run the Sheridan-Verplank classifier on my own week. Help me: (1) write descriptions of eight of my real AI interactions and classify each as Level I, II, or III; (2) implement the gap flag that fires when verify time exceeds two minutes while level is I or II; (3) plot them on the calibrated diagonal so I can see exactly which interactions fall below it.

props.command: The usage-versus-supervision grid clarified why Priya's Interaction B, Level II capability with Level I supervision, sits dangerously below the diagonal. I want to run the Sheridan-Verplank classifier on my own week. Help me: (1) write descriptions of eight of my real AI interactions and classify each as Level I, II, or III; (2) implement the gap flag that fires when verify time exceeds two minutes while level is I or II; (3) plot them on the calibrated diagonal so I can see exactly which interactions fall below it.

beats.narration_text: Nik Bear Brown. Build it with a CLI, then take it apart.

Not all AI use is the same. Copying a variable name with AI assistance is nothing like asking AI to propose a system architecture. But in practice, people apply the same supervision level to both — a quick glance, maybe a nod, and move on. Sheridan and Verplank catalogued ten levels of automation back in 1978. Applied to AI, three of them matter: copy-paste use, research-level use, and true collaboration. The question is whether your supervision level is calibrated to the usage level — or whether there is a dangerous gap.
beats.visual_intent: title card: supervision calibration as the core problem; Priya example — same interface, three different tasks, same supervision

beats.narration_text: Ask for the classifier. In the terminal: claude — write a Python script that defines eight AI interaction descriptions, calls the Anthropic API to classify each as Level I, II, or III per the Sheridan-Verplank automation framework, and returns JSON with level, supervisory demand, verify time in minutes, and a gap flag that fires when verify exceeds two minutes and level is I or II. Use claude-3-5-haiku.

Read it before trusting it. The gap flag is one line: verify time exceeds two minutes AND level is I or II. That condition catches the most dangerous case — high-consequence AI use paired with minimal supervision. It is not about effort; it is about mismatch.

props.code:
# supervision_classifier.py — Claude Code output
import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

def classify_interaction(description):
    resp = client.messages.create(
        model='claude-3-5-haiku-20241022',
        max_tokens=512,
        messages=[{'role':'user','content':
            f'Classify this AI interaction per Sheridan-Verplank '
            f'framework (Level I/II/III): {description}'
            f' Return JSON: level, supervisory_demand, verify_time_minutes'
        }]
    )
    data = json.loads(resp.content[0].text)
    # gap_flag = verify_time_minutes > 2 and level in ["Level I","Level II"]
    data['gap_flag'] = (
        data['verify_time_minutes'] > 2 and
        data['level'] in ['Level I','Level II']
    )
    return data

beats.narration_text: Run it. The visualization maps Priya's three interactions on a usage-level versus supervision-level grid. Interaction A — rename a variable — lands on the calibrated diagonal. That is fine. Interaction B — cite a market size — uses Level II capability but Level I supervision. Below the diagonal. Interaction C — propose an architecture — uses Level III capability with Level I supervision. Far below. Priya is using the same glance for decisions with very different stakes.
beats.visual_intent: Manim B04_SupervisionGrid: 2-axis grid, calibrated diagonal, danger corner, Priya's 3 dots below diagonal

beats.narration_text: The classifier is flagging correctly but the output table is hard to read. Revise: add a column that names the supervisory demand explicitly, and color-code the gap flag.

props.command:
claude "update supervision_classifier.py:
  -> add supervisory_demand column to output table
  -> mark gap_flag=True rows with [GAP] prefix
  -> re-run with same 8 descriptions"

beats.role_note: slate — fill with updated table or screen-rec
beats.narration_text: Re-run. The table now shows the supervisory demand label alongside the flag. Four of the eight interactions carry gap flags. That means four decisions made at AI speed are not being verified at the appropriate level.
beats.visual_intent: updated output table with supervisory_demand column and gap flag markers

beats.narration_text: The same chat interface carries Level I, II, and III interactions. What changes is the supervision level required — and whether the person using it knows that. Sheridan-Verplank gives a vocabulary for naming the gap. The gap flag is where that vocabulary bites.
beats.visual_intent: summary card: same interface — three different supervision requirements; the calibrated diagonal as the target

beats.narration_text: Your move: run the classifier on your own last ten AI interactions. For any that come back Level III with less than two minutes of verification time, ask what you actually checked. That is the gap. And the gap is what this course is about closing.
beats.visual_intent: next-steps card: 1) classify your last 10 AI interactions, 2) audit verify time for Level III results

beats.title: Liam verdict — ClaudeVerdictArtifact
beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. Here's what the body just demonstrated. The same chat interface carries Level I, II, and III interactions. What changes is the supervision level required — and whether the person using it knows that. Sheridan-Verplank gives a vocabulary for naming the gap. The gap flag is where that vocabulary bites. Your move: run the classifier on your own last ten AI interactions. For any that come back Level III with less than two minutes of verification time, ask what you actually checked. That is the gap. And the gap is what this course is about closing.

beats.title: Your turn handoff — ClaudeComposerAsk
beats.narration_text: Take this prompt, run it on your own — pick any cancer type or clinical scenario you know about and ask how this mechanism applies there.

props.command: Explain how [Three Levels of AI Supervision — The Sheridan-Verplank Framework] applies to a specific cancer type or clinical case you're studying. What proteins are involved, what goes wrong at the molecular level, and what is the therapeutic or diagnostic significance?
